misconceptionSummary: Students read base-2 digits as if they were base-10, treating 1010₂ as one thousand and ten.

lines.text: Each column is double the one to its right

steps.narration: In base two, each column is worth twice the column to its right — instead of ten times as in base ten. The columns from right to left are one, two, four, eight, sixteen, and so on.
Multiply each binary digit by its column value and add the results. One times eight, zero times four, one times two, one times one — that gives eleven.

visuals.ruleText: Base 2 uses only the digits 0 and 1 — no digit can equal or exceed the base
visuals.subText: Base 10 uses 0–9; base 2 uses 0–1; base 16 uses 0–9 and A–F

steps.narration: Each base only uses digits from zero up to one less than the base. Base two allows only zero and one. Base sixteen needs sixteen symbols, so it borrows letters A through F for ten through fifteen.

visuals.label: Multiply each digit by its column power of 2 → add

steps.narration: One zero one one in base two equals eleven in base ten. The column values — powers of two — do the conversion.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: reading 1011₂ as 'one thousand and eleven'. The subscript 2 means every column is a power of 2, not 10. Multiply each digit by its power of 2 and add.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The base changes the column values. In base 2, columns are 1, 2, 4, 8 — not 1, 10, 100, 1000. Always multiply by the correct power of the base.
